coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal process via sodium citrate and NH4HCO3 for sensitive detection of temperature and sunset yellow
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Huan
Long, Yuwei
Li, Hongxi
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Yang, Jidong
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by an one-pot portable hydrothermal method from sodium citrate and NH4HCO3. The obtained CDs were found to show high fluorescence quantum yield (31%), excitation-wavelength independence, outstanding water-soluble, excellent chemical and optical stability. Interestingly, it was discovered that sunset yellow (SY) was sensitive and effective in quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this observation, CDs could be applied as a novel fluoescent probe for detection of SY. The analytical potential of this probe was evaluated and illustrated a good linear relationship at the concentration range from 0.25 to 56.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit (3σ/k) of 74.49 nmol L−1. Meanwhile, the practicality of this nanosensor for SY determination in real samples was validated with good accuracy and precision. In addtion, long time UV radiation and high ionic strength hardly influenced on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased at higher temperature and the fluorescence was recovered when temperature back to 5 °C. Therefore, CDs could be also employed as an fluorescent temperature sensor with significant sensitivity, linearity and remarkable reversibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sunset yellow
Temperature
Fluorescence quenching
Static quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of CDs for SY detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (inset: the histogram of CDs size distributions). (B) FTIR of CDs. (C) XRD spectrum of CDs.
(A) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Abs), Excitation spectrum (Ex), Emission spectrum (Em).

The study on stability of CDs. (A) The stability in a salty medium. (B) Photostability of CDs. (C) pH stability. (D) Temperature stability. (E) Temperature reversibility study of CDs between temperature 5 °C to 75 °C. V CDs = 0.2 mL, λ ex = 340 nm.
(A) The effect of pH. (B) The effect of CDs volume. (C) The effect of reaction time. (D) The effect of temperature. V CDs = 0.4 mL, c SY = 4.0 × 10−5 mol L−1, V BR = 0.7 mL, 6.0 BR buffer solution.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations of SY. (B) Calibration curve. V CDs = 0.4 mL, a–g c SY: 0; 1.6; 2.4; 3.2; 4.0; 4.8; 5.6 × 10−5 mol L−1, V BR = 0.7 mL, 6.0 BR buffer solution.

(A) Stern-Volmer plots for the CDs-SY system at three different temperatures in pH 6.0 BR buffer solution. (B) Absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-SY system. (C) Fluorescence emission decay curves of CDs and CDs-SY system.
Schematic illustration of synthesis process of CDs and detection principle for SY and temperature.
Comparison of this method with other methods for determination of SY.

row.entry: High performance but the solid phase extraction column is expensive
Sensitive yet require much time and steps to fabricate functionalized electrodes
High separation efficiency yet poor reproducibility
Selective but the too long operation time are needed

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of foreign substances (c SY = 18.1 μg mL−1).
Determination results of SY in drink water.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal process via sodium citrate and NH4HCO3 for sensitive detection of temperature and sunset yellow

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Chongqing Three Gorges University, Wanzhou, Chongqing 404100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by an one-pot portable hydrothermal method from sodium citrate and NH4HCO3. The obtained CDs were found to show high fluorescence quantum yield (31%), excitation-wavelength independence, outstanding water-soluble, excellent chemical and optical stability. Interestingly, it was discovered that sunset yellow (SY) was sensitive and effective in quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Based on this observation, CDs could be applied as a novel fluoescent probe for detection of SY. The analytical potential of this probe was evaluated and illustrated a good linear relationship at the concentration range from 0.25 to 56.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit (3σ/k) of 74.49 nmol L−1. Meanwhile, the practicality of this nanosensor for SY determination in real samples was validated with good accuracy and precision. In addtion, long time UV radiation and high ionic strength hardly influenced on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased at higher temperature and the fluorescence was recovered when temperature back to 5 °C. Therefore, CDs could be also employed as an fluorescent temperature sensor with significant sensitivity, linearity and remarkable reversibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic food colorants have been widely used as additives in the food industry due to their intriguing characteristics including low cost production, excellent color uniformity as well as high stability. Sunset yellow (SY) is one of common synthesis azo dye added in the drinks and foods, which contains central aromatic groups (phenyl and naphthyl rings linked via an azo group) and two solubilizing sulfonate groups attached to either end of the molecule [1,2]. Because of its azo (NN) functional group and aromatic ring structure, SY is potentially able to affect human health. Several studies have revealed that SY may causes adverse health effects such as allergies, migraines, eczema, anxiety, diarrhea and even cancer when it is excessively consumed [3,4]. Therefore, the Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) and World Health Organization (WHO) recommended daily intake of SY below than 2.5 mg/kg [5]. Facing with increasing legal restrictions, it is indispensable to strictly control and regulate the content of SY in the drinks and foods. Up to now, a myriad of methods, such as immunoassay [6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8], electrochemical [9–12], capillary zone electrophoresis [13], voltammetry [14,15] and spectrophotometry [16–19] have been proposed for detection of SY. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy has been considered as a powerful optical technology that can provide a flexible, sensitive, facile detection method. Over the past several years, considerable efforts have been devoted to explore efficient fluorescent sensor for SY detection [19–21]. Nevertheless, sensitive and selective detection methods with ideal environmental safety, simple operability, and low-cost are still highly desirable for SY analysis in the food field.

Temperature is one of fundamental physiological parameter that affects the behavior of physical, chemical, and biological systems in a wide variety of ways. Accordingly, the accurate measurement of temperature and its gradient inside a living cell can promote advancements in cell biology and biomedicine [22]. To the best of our knowledge, few analysis protocols have been reported for temperature detection. To this end, the development of facile and inexpensive methods for rapid and sensitive detection of temperature are in ever increasing demand.

Carbon dots (CDs), since it was discovered in 2004, have garnered increasing attention due to their outstanding merits, such as chemical inertness, easy fabrication, low photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [23]. In the light of distinct performance, copious researchers have focused on the synthesis, characterization and practical applications of CDs. So far, CDs could be synthesized through two main methods: top-down and bottom-up approaches [24]. Top-down ways consist of laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation of graphite, electrochemical treatment of multiwalled carbon nanotubes as well as chemical oxidation of activated carbon etc [25–29]. Bottom-up methods including hydrothermal method [30], microwave and ultrasonication method etc [31,32]. Until now, a great deal of efforts have been adopted to their applications in sensor [33], bioimaging [34], photocatalyst as well as drug delivery [35,36]. Based on changes of fluorescence intensity, CDs have been used for the detection of Fe3+ [37], Al3+ [38], Hg2+ [39], I− [40], Cr2O7 2− [41], P2O7 4 − [42], biothiol [43], ascorbic acid [44], DNA [45], guanine [46], H2O2 [47], glucose [48]. However, CDs are rarely used for detection of SY. Therefore, the detection of SY based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs is innovative and meaningful.

In this work, CDs were synthesized by simply mixing sodium citrate and NH4HCO3, and then acted as a sensitive temperature sensor and an high-performance fluorescent probe for detection of SY. The synthesis and detection protocols were illustrated in scheme 1 . The morphology, surface functional groups, optical properties of CDs were investigated systematically. The as-prepared CDs exhibited high fluorescence quantum yield (31%), excitation-wavelength independence, temperature-sensitivity. As an effective fluorescent probe for SY, CDs possessed remarkable selectivity and sensitivity with the linear range of 0.25 μmol L−1–56.0 μmol L−1 and the detection limit for SY was 74.49 nmol L−1 (3σ/k). As temperature sensor, the fluorescence intensity linearly decreased within the range from 5 °C to 75 °C with excellent sensitivity and significant reversibility.

The sodium citrate was purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). NH4HCO3 was obtained from Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Sunset yellow was supplied by Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, Co., Ltd., China). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent and directly used without additional purification. The BR buffer solution of various pH were prepared by mixing the mixed acid (composed of 2.71 mL 85% H3PO4, 2.36 mL HAc and 2.47 g H3BO3) with 0.2 mol L−1 NaOH in different ratio. The solutions were prepared using ultrapure water by a Millipore system with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ cm.

The fluorescence spectrums were acquired by an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) using a 1 cm path length. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Hitachi U-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The morphology and sizes of CDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Kyoto, Japan) was used to identify functional groups of the as-prepared CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was performed with AXS-D8 X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potentials were measured by dynamic laser light scattering (ZEN3600, Malvern). A FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) was employed to measure fluorescence lifetime at room temperature. The pH values of solutions were made with a PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd., China).

The CDs were synthesized according to the previous method with slight modification [49]. In a typical experiment, 10.0 mL of a clear and transparent solution containing 0.29 g sodium citrate and 0.095 g NH4HCO3 transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 3 h, and the color of the solution changed gradually from colorless to yellow. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the synthesized CDs solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then was dialyzed against ultrapure water (500 Da, molecular weight cut off) for 24 h. The obtained CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further research.

In a typical run, amounts of 0.7 mL BR buffer solution and 0.4 mL of purified CDs solution were placed in a series of 10.0 mL colorimetric tubes. Then, an appropriate amount of SY standard solution or sample solution was added and final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 10.0 mL with ultrapure water. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the mixed solution was measured using a 1 cm path length quartz cell under the fluorescence excitation wavelength at 340 nm with slit width of 10 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF) can be calculated as: ΔF = F 0 − F, where F refers to the fluorescence of reaction system and F 0 for reagent blank.

The effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also investigated. A constant temperature water cycle was applied to monitor change of fluorescence intensity of CDs. In order to demonstrate the reversibility and restorability, a strategy was designed to observe the fluorescence variation with a pendular value of temperature. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased at higher temperature and the fluorescence was recovered when temperature back to 5 °C.

Quantum yield (QY) of CDs was investigated by comparison of the photoluminescence of CDs and absorbance values with quinine sulfate. In order to minimize re-absorption effect, the absorbance of CDs solution kept below 0.10 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (QY = 54%). The quantum yield was calculated by the following equation:
Φ
u
=
Φ
s
F
u
F
s
A
s
A
u
where Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, F stands for the integrated emission intensity, A represents the optical density at excitation wavelength. The subscript ‘s’ refers to standard with known QY and ‘u’ is the unknown samples. The QY of CDs was calculated to be 31%.

The CDs were systematically characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 A, CDs were uniform, well-dispersed and no aggregation emerged. The sizes of CDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2.4 nm–4.6 nm with an average size of 3.2 nm. For a better insight of CDs, the FTIR was employed to identify functional groups on the surfece of CDs. As revealed in Fig. 1B, the peak centered at 3411 cm−1 can be attributed to the N-H stretching vibration. The band around 3219 cm−1 was assigned to the OH stretching vibration. Peaks at 1687 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibration and CN stretching vibration, respectively. The results indicated that the as-synthesized CDs were functionalized by carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups, which would impart excellent solubility in water without the need of further chemical modification. In addition, the XRD profile of CDs (Fig. 1C) exhibited a wider diffraction peak located at around 23.9°, which may be attributed to highly disordered carbon structure.

The optical properties of CDs were carried out by UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the emission peaks of CDs remained unchanged even though the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 nm to 390 nm, and the highest fluorescence intensity located at excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The emission spectra of CDs exhibited excitation independent feature which indicate CDs have a prominent fluorescence property. This behavior was due to the different emissive sites on CDs and the different sizes of CDs. The emission peaks of CDs at various excitation wavelengths did not shift, which make it clear that CDs is homogenous. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2B) exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 233 and 339 nm. The peak at 233 nm can be assigned to the π → π∗ transitions of the aromatic CC and CC in sp2 hybrid domains, which can not produce observable fluorescence signal [50]. The peak at 339 nm was ascribed to n → π∗ transitions of CO or COH bond in sp3 hybrid regions, originates from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can result in strong fluorescence [51].

Stability is one particularly important property of nanomaterials. The synthesized CDs are observed to be very stable even the concentration of NaCl as high as 1.0 mol L−1 (Fig. 3 A) and the fluorescence intensity remains unchanged under continuous irradiation for 60 min (Fig. 3B), which indicate CDs possess excellent photostability and salt-tolerance. In addition, fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the pH increasing from 2.0 to 7.0, then it slightly decreases when pH > 7.0 (Fig. 3C). Under acidic condition, the amine moieties on the surface of CDs are protonated to ammonium salt (-NH3 +) and the passivation effective may be susceptible. On the other hand, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs are prone to protonate, which leads to the aggregation of CDs, also resulting in the fluorescence quenching [52]. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases, and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs are easily to deprotonate, leading to the fluorescence intensity of CDs slightly decreases in the solution of pH 7.0–10.0. Therefore, the pH dependency of the fluorescence of CDs is possibly ascribed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs such as amide and carboxylic groups. This phenomenon further confirms the existence of carbonyl groups and amino on the surface of CDs. Meanwhile, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases gradually with the increase of temperature (Fig. 3D) and the fluorescence is recovered when temperature back to 5 °C (Fig. 3E). The fluorescence intensity is proportional to temperature in the range of 5–75 °C, which suggests CDs can be considered as an effective temperature sensor. There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the temperature within the range of 5–75 °C.
F = 2225.54 - 20.87
T (
R
2
= 0.998 ,
T : 5 ° C - 75 ° C )
where F is the fluorescence intensity and T represents the temperature. The temperature induced fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs is still unknown. The research have proposed which may be attributed to the synergistic effect of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrogen bonds on the surface of CDs [53]. Meanwhile, several studies have also suggested that it is related to the the activation of nonradiative relaxation channels of surface states [54–56].

In order to obtain the excellent sensitivity for the determination of SY, the detection conditions, such as pH, the volume of CDs, reaction time and temperature were investigated. The effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was studied in the range of 2.0–10.0 using Britton-Robinson buffer. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF = F 0 − F, where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of SY) of CDs had a tendency to increase at higher pH and reached the maximum at pH 6.0. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased due to the instability at lower pH values. With pH increasing from 6.0 to 10.0, the zeta potential of CDs changed from −5.81 to −15.4 mV. In addition, the SY dissociated into anion when pH > 6.1 (pKa1 = 6.1) [17]. These results indicated that the electrostatic repulsion increased at higher pH value, which hindered the interaction of the CDs with SY. Considering the balance between the above two effects, a weak acid condition (pH 6.0) was adopted in the sensing. In addition, the effect of CDs volume should be considered. As shown in Fig. 4B, the ΔF slightly increased with increasing the volume of CDs and remained unchanged in 0.4 mL. Thus, 0.4 mL CDs solution was selected as the optimum value for all experiments. Subsequently, the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, it was clear that the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached maximum when the reaction time was 5 min. Hence, fluorescence spectra were recorded at 5 min after the addition of SY. Finally, temperature had a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, so the effect of temperature on the detection of SY was also explored. As shown in Fig. 4D, temperature hardly affected the detection of SY, thus all detection on SY should be performed at room temperature.

Under the above optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased with the increasing of SY concentration (Fig. 5 A). The surface of CDs contains abundant amino groups and SY contains the phenolic hydroxyl group. Therefore, SY may link to the surface of CDs through hydrogen bond and quenched the fluorescence of CDs [57]. In the meantime, the zeta potential measurements were also investigated. The zeta potential of CDs was −5.81 mV at pH 6.0, which subsequently changed to −7.29 mV after the addition of SY. The results also suggested that SY effectively coordinated to the surface of CDs. Fig. 5B revealed a good linear correlation between ΔF and the concentration of SY in the range of 0.25–56.0 μmol L−1, and the calibration curve can be expressed as ΔF = 636.78c − 526.76, with the correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9990, where ΔF = F 0 − F is the difference between the fluorescence intensity in the absence of SY (F 0) and in its presence (F), and c is the concentration of SY. The detection limit for SY was obtained of 74.49 nmol L−1 calculated based on (3σ/k) (σ is the standard deviation, and k is the slope of the calibration curve), which is much more sensitive, facile than other reported analytical methods for SY detection in the literature (shown in Table 1 ).

The mechanism for fluorescence quenching, mainly via static quenching and/or dynamic quenching, can be distinguished by investigating the variation of the quenching constant at different temperatures. The quenching constants increase with increasing temperature for dynamic quenching, while when it came to static quenching and appeared reverse effect [58]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be analyzed quantitatively at different temperatures (278, 298, 318 k) with the Stern-Volmer equation [59]:
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
q
τ
0
[ Q ] = 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence and absence of quencher, respectively, Kq stands for the quenching constant, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, τ 0 refers to the fluorescence lifetime in the absence of quencher, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Fig. 6 A showed the Stern-Volmer plots of F 0 /F versus [Q] at three different temperatures. It manifested that the quenching constant decreased with a rise in temperature, which indicated that the quenching mechanism of system was static quenching.

The absorption spectrum was one of the forceful way to distinguish static quenching and dynamic quenching. As plotted in Fig. 6B, the maximum absorption peak of CDs was located at 339 nm. It was clear that absorption intensity of CDs-SY system enhanced and absorption peak were located at 320 nm and 481 nm. The results demonstrated that the maximum absorption wavelength of CDs appeared blue shift phenomenon and exhibited another absorption peak at 481 nm. Hence, the CDs-SY system was significant reason that causes fluorescence quenching.

As we all know, the most efficient method to distinguish dynamic quenching or static quenching was fluorescence lifetime. As for static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime did not change (τ 0/τ = 1), where τ 0 and τ were fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively. Inversely, for dynamic quenching, τ 0 /τ = F 0 /F, and the lifetime decreased after addition of the quencher [60]. As displayed in Fig. 6C, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs-SY system were 6.52 ns and 6.44 ns, respectively, τ 0 /τ ≈ 1, which certified that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs for SY detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effect of different interfering substances on detection of SY were investigated. If the tolerable mass ratios for interference in relative error of <5%, the foreign substances were considered as have less interference for determination of SY. As shown in Table 2 , many cations, anions as well as amino acids did not interfere with the detection of SY and some other organic compounds also did not interfere. Importantly, the relative error in the range of 95.0–105.0%, suggesting the method not only possessed good selectivity, but also could be applied to detect SY in real samples.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the mentioned method for detection of SY in real sample, the fluorescent probe was applied to determinate SY in drink water. The drink water was purchased from a local supermarket (Yonghui supermarket, Chongqing, China) and used directly without any treatment. In short, 0.4 mL CDs, 0.7 mL pH 6.0 BR buffer solution, and 0.4 mL SY solution were successively added to 10.0 mL colorimetric tubes, and then diluted to the mark with ultrapure water. After incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded and the concentration of SY was calculated by the above regression equation. The results were presented in Table 3 , the recoveries of SY were in the range of 98.6–102.6%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was around 1.9–3.4%. These results suggested that analytical performance for detection of SY in drink water was satisfactory.

In summary, a facile and one-step hydrothermal method had been developed for the synthesis of CDs on the basis of the previously reported preparative approaches with slight modification [49]. Given the high fluorescence quantum yield (31%), excitation-wavelength independence, outstanding water-soluble, excellent chemical and optical stability, the as-synthesized CDs can be potentially adopted to applications of chemical sensing. In this work, the as-prepared NCDs have been successfully used for determination of SY and temperature with high sensitivity and selectivity. Based on SY-induced fluorescence quenching of as-prepared CDs, a good linearity for detecting SY was attained over the concentration range from 0.25 to 56.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 74.49 nmol L−1. In addition, fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually with the increase of temperature and the fluorescence was recovered when temperature back to starting temperature. For the easy fabrication of stable and strong fluorescence of CDs, our study may offered a potential avenue for application of CDs in chemical sensing and other purposes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21705132 and 21475014) and the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC).
